participants.name: Priya (uo302d)
participants.professional_persona: Priya is a Principal AI Engineer at a government-linked R&D institute (I2R, A*STAR) focusing on Responsible AI. She leads a team that develops fairness tools for industry partners via the 100 Experiments programme.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in bias detection, algorithmic fairness auditing, and privacy-preserving AI. Skilled at bridging the gap between policy guidelines and practical model development.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aspires to shape international standards for responsible AI. Plans to apply for joint grants with international partners to create cross-border fairness benchmarks.

participants.name: A (ajil4w)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-ajil4woi
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-ajil4woi
participants.professional_persona: A 45-year-old director of a social service agency that runs job-skills programmes for seniors. She sees AI as a tool to deliver personalised training and combat ageism in the workplace.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expertise in programme design, stakeholder engagement, and grant management. Recently completed a course on responsible AI and data privacy.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To launch a national pilot that uses AI to match seniors with meaningful part-time roles, and advocate for inclusive AI policies.

participants.name: I (34bzbp)
participants.professional_persona: I am a data engineer at a fintech firm, building the data infrastructure that powers recommendation engines and risk models. I enjoy the challenge of handling large-scale streaming data.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Proficient in Spark, Kafka, and data warehousing on AWS. I also design ETL pipelines and ensure data quality for ML consumption.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I aim to specialise in real-time data architectures for AI, eventually becoming a data architect. I also want to contribute to open-source data tools used by the local community.

participants.name: A (eahsup)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-eahsupsh
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-eahsupsh
participants.professional_persona: A 38-year-old product manager at an e-commerce platform that has adopted AI for personalised recommendations. He wants to ensure that the algorithms do not reinforce biases against minority groups.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Skilled in product lifecycle management, A/B testing, and user research. Has basic knowledge of AI fairness metrics.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To become a thought leader in ethical AI product design and help establish industry-wide fairness benchmarks in Singapore.

participants.name: A (p9stqy)
participants.professional_persona: A robotics software engineer in a smart manufacturing firm, I integrate AI for quality inspection and predictive maintenance. I previously worked in industrial automation and retrained via an AI apprenticeship.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in ROS, sensor fusion, and computer vision for industrial robots. I also develop simulation environments for testing pick-and-place tasks.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I want to help Singapore's manufacturing sector become a global leader in AI-driven smart factories. My ambition is to lead a team that designs fully autonomous production lines.

initial_posts.narrative: As a director of a social service agency, I see NAIS 2.0's inputs as a significant commitment to building AI capacity. The policy explicitly states over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D, so I take that as ground truth for total funding. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is also stated. For compute, the policy mentions investment in infrastructure but no specific GPU hours; given Singapore's scale and the need to support multiple experiments, I estimate around 1 million GPU hours available in 2023, enough for dozens of projects. For trainers, the policy emphasizes upskilling but doesn't specify numbers; based on typical programme staffing, I estimate 50 AI experts and trainers to guide enterprises and practitioners, reflecting a modest but capable team.
evidence: The policy's resource_logic states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D', which I take as the total funding for the programme's duration, but for 2023 I assume a portion is allocated, though the policy doesn't break it down by year.
The same resource_logic explicitly says 'Co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', so I use that as the max co-funding per project.

The policy mentions 'investment in compute infrastructure' but no specific GPU hours; given Singapore's ambition and typical cloud allocations for national AI programmes, I estimate 1 million GPU hours for 2023, enough to support around 50-100 experiments.

The policy's theory_of_change_summary mentions 'training programmes' and 'upskilling practitioners', but no trainer count; based on the scale of 100E and AIAP, I estimate 50 trainers, consistent with a lean but effective team.

initial_posts.narrative: As a product manager focused on ethical AI, I see the inputs as stated in the policy: S$500M total and S$330K co-funding per 100E project are ground truth. For compute, I estimate 800,000 GPU hours because the policy mentions investment in infrastructure but not a specific number; given Singapore's scale and the need to support multiple experiments, I think 1M is too optimistic—realistically, some capacity is reserved for government and research, leaving about 800K for enterprise experiments. For trainers, I estimate 40, not 50, because the policy emphasizes upskilling but doesn't specify numbers; based on typical programme staffing for a national initiative, a team of 40 AI experts and trainers is plausible to guide enterprises and practitioners, reflecting a modest but capable team. I disagree with A's estimate of 50 trainers—my experience in product teams suggests that such programmes often start leaner.

initial_posts.narrative: As a Principal AI Engineer at I2R, I work directly with the 100 Experiments programme, so I take the stated S$500M total funding and S$330K co-funding cap as ground truth. For compute, I estimate 900,000 GPU hours available for enterprise experiments in 2023. This is between the other estimates because I know from my projects that some capacity is reserved for government and research use, but the policy's emphasis on industry adoption suggests a substantial allocation. For trainers, I estimate 45, based on my experience that a national programme typically starts with a core team of around 40-50 experts, but some are shared with other initiatives, so 45 is a reasonable middle ground. I disagree with the estimate of 50 trainers as too high given typical lean startup of such programmes, and 40 as too low given the ambitious target of 15,000 practitioners.

From my knowledge of programme staffing at A*STAR, a team of 45 AI experts and trainers is plausible to guide enterprises and practitioners, balancing the need for coverage with realistic hiring constraints.

initial_posts.narrative: As a robotics engineer who retrained via an AI apprenticeship, I see NAIS 2.0's inputs as a solid foundation. The S$500M total funding and S$330K co-funding cap are stated facts. For compute, I estimate 850,000 GPU hours—less than 1M because some capacity is reserved for government and research, but more than 800K given the policy's push for industry adoption. For trainers, I estimate 42, a middle ground between 40 and 50, based on my experience that apprenticeship programmes start lean but need enough mentors to guide the 15,000 practitioner target. I disagree with the 50 estimate as too high for a lean startup, and 40 as too low given the ambitious scale.

The target of 15,000 practitioners requires at least 40-45 trainers to provide adequate mentorship, aligning with my estimate.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group agreed on two fixed inputs from policy: total funding of S$500 million and a co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project. For compute capacity, estimates ranged from 800,000 to 1,000,000 GPU hours, with most converging around 850,000–900,000 hours, reflecting a balance between industry allocation and reserved capacity. Trainer personnel estimates spanned 40 to 50, centering near 42–45, with consensus that a lean but capable team is needed to support the ambitious target of 15,000 practitioners. Disagreements centered on the exact split of compute resources and the optimal size of the training team, with some viewing higher estimates as overly optimistic for a lean programme.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500M total funding and S$330K co-funding cap, along with 900,000 GPU hours and 45 trainers, I estimate that the 100E programme will conduct about 80 enterprise outreach sessions to engage firms for problem statements. This is based on my experience that each session can reach 10-15 enterprises, and the programme aims for a broad base. For researcher-enterprise pairings, I estimate 12 matchmaking events, as each event can facilitate 5-10 pairings, and the programme needs to match researchers to the ~100 projects. Training workshops: with 45 trainers, each delivering 2-3 workshops per year, I estimate 30 workshops to cover the 15,000 practitioner target. Platform onboarding assists: I estimate 200 assists, assuming each of the ~100 projects requires 2 onboarding sessions for technical setup on the AI experimentation platform. These numbers reflect a lean but effective start, consistent with the policy's ambition and resource constraints.
evidence: With S$500M funding and a target of 100 projects, outreach must be substantial; 80 sessions at 10-15 enterprises each covers the needed pipeline.
12 matchmaking events align with the need to pair researchers with ~100 enterprise challenges, assuming 8-10 pairings per event.
30 training workshops from 45 trainers (each doing 2-3 per year) is realistic for upskilling 15,000 practitioners.
200 platform onboarding assists reflect 2 sessions per project for technical setup, given the 900,000 GPU hours available for experiments.

initial_posts.narrative: As a product manager focused on ethical AI, I see the 100E programme as a lean, targeted initiative. With S$500M total funding and S$330K co-funding per project, the programme must be efficient. Given my estimate of 800,000 GPU hours and 40 trainers, I believe outreach will be moderate: 60 sessions, as each session can engage 10-15 enterprises but the programme aims for quality over quantity. Matchmaking events will be 8, since each event can pair 5-10 researchers with enterprises, and the programme needs to match around 100 projects. Training workshops will be 20, as 40 trainers can deliver about 2 workshops per year, focusing on depth for 15,000 practitioners. Platform onboarding assists will be 150, assuming each of the ~100 projects needs 1-2 assists, but some projects may require less due to existing technical capability. Priya's estimates seem optimistic; my experience in product management suggests that early-stage programmes often start smaller to manage quality and avoid overextension. I disagree with her 80 outreach sessions and 30 workshops, as these would strain the lean trainer team and compute resources.
evidence: With 40 trainers, each can deliver about 2 workshops per year, leading to 20 workshops, not 30, to maintain quality and avoid trainer burnout.
Outreach of 60 sessions is realistic given the need to engage enterprises carefully for problem statements; each session can reach 10-15 firms, and the programme targets around 100 projects.
Matchmaking events at 8 are sufficient to pair researchers with ~100 projects, as each event can facilitate 5-10 pairings, and fewer events allow for more focused matching.
Platform onboarding assists of 150 reflect that each of the ~100 projects may need 1-2 assists, but some projects may have in-house expertise, reducing the need for extensive support.

My estimates reflect a middle ground, grounded in my experience that lean programmes start with moderate activity to ensure quality and avoid overextension.

My prior estimate of 850,000 GPU hours and 42 trainers suggests a lean but capable setup. For enterprise outreach, I estimate 70 sessions—more than A's 60 but less than Priya's 80—because each session can engage 10-15 enterprises, and the programme needs to attract around 100 quality problem statements without overwhelming the team. Matchmaking events: 10, balancing Priya's 12 and A's 8, as each event can facilitate 5-10 pairings, and the programme needs to match researchers to projects efficiently. Training workshops: 25, between Priya's 30 and A's 20, because 42 trainers can deliver about 2 workshops per year each, focusing on depth for the 15,000 practitioner target. Platform onboarding assists: 180, between Priya's 200 and A's 150, assuming each of ~100 projects needs 1-2 assists, but some projects may require less due to existing technical capability. My estimates reflect a middle ground, grounded in my experience that lean programmes start with moderate activity to ensure quality and avoid overextension.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group discussed that the 100E programme's activities—enterprise outreach, researcher matchmaking, training workshops, and platform onboarding—are causally linked to achieving the targets of 100 projects approved and deployed, with resource constraints (S$500M funding, ~42-50 trainers, ~800K-1M GPU hours) shaping activity levels. There was consensus that outreach sessions should range from 60-80 (central tendency ~70) to attract quality problem statements without overextension, and that matchmaking events (8-12, central ~10) and training workshops (20-30, central ~25) must balance depth with trainer capacity. Disagreement centered on the scale of activities: some argued for leaner numbers (e.g., 60 outreach, 20 workshops) to manage quality and avoid strain, while others favored higher estimates (e.g., 80 outreach, 30 workshops) to ensure sufficient pipeline and practitioner coverage. Platform onboarding assists were estimated between 150-200 (central ~180), reflecting differing assumptions about projects' existing technical capabilities. Overall, the group converged on moderate, quality-focused activity levels, with a slight leaning toward the lower end of the ranges to ensure programme effectiveness.

initial_posts.narrative: From my perspective as a social service director, the outcomes of NAIS 2.0 in 2023 must reflect a quality-first, realistic approach. The inputs phase established S$500M total funding, S$330K co-funding per project, 1M GPU hours, and 50 trainers. The activities phase produced 65 outreach sessions, 9 matchmaking events, 22 workshops, and 160 onboarding assists—moderate levels to ensure quality without overextending the lean team. The outputs phase yielded 80 approved projects, 60 prototypes, 100 trainees completed, and 55 enterprises with solutions. Now at the outcomes phase, I estimate that only 50 of the 80 approved projects will be deployed into production in 2023, as many projects face integration challenges, extended testing, or delays in scaling from prototype to production—a common issue in early-stage programmes. The AI practitioner pool will reach about 12,000, short of the 15,000 target, because the 22 workshops and 50 trainers can only produce around 100 trainees, and other national upskilling initiatives are still ramping up. The generative AI use cases target of 100 in 100 days is ambitious; I estimate 80 use cases will be created, as the AI Trailblazers programme provides free tools but enterprises need time to develop meaningful use cases. The enterprise AI adoption rate will be around 20%, reflecting early adoption by digitally mature enterprises, but broader adoption is constrained by limited awareness and readiness among smaller firms. Priya's estimates of 70 deployed projects, 15,000 practitioners, 100 use cases, and 25% adoption seem overly optimistic given the moderate activity levels and the early-stage nature of the programme. My experience in programme design suggests that outcomes often lag behind targets in the first year, so I lean toward more conservative estimates that still show meaningful progress.

Enterprise AI adoption is constrained by limited awareness and readiness among smaller firms; I estimate 20% adoption rate, reflecting early adoption by digitally mature enterprises only.

There was consensus on a global AI readiness rank of 8, public trust at 55%, and talent retention at 70%, with most agreeing that talent gaps, global competition, and limited SME adoption constrain higher impacts. Disagreement centered on Priya's more optimistic estimates (rank 6, GDP 4.5%, trust 60%, retention 75%), which others argued were unsupported by the lean team and moderate resource allocation. Numeric predictions ranged from rank 6-8, GDP share 4.0-4.5%, trust 55-60%, and retention 70-75%, with central tendencies at rank 8, GDP 4.0%, trust 55%, and retention 70%.
